title: Reclaiming Practice After a Bad Teacher
dek: When a teacher betrays your trust, your body carries the memory long before your mind can catch up. This is not moral failure but biology - the masseter clenches, the psoas tightens, and the diaphragm resists its full breath. Drawing on somatic wisdom from teachers like Robert Augustus Masters and Resmaa Menakem, and the grounding power of herbs, you learn to listen to your body's fierce guidance and reclaim your practice with tenderness and presence.
bodyMarkdown:
## Introduction: The Somatic Reality of Betrayal by a Teacher


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:42.975Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You feel it first in the back of your throat - a sudden tightening that curls like a snake, constricting the pharynx and triggering a dry, rasping swallow. It’s as if the masseter muscles, those powerful workhorses clenching your jaw, refuse to release their grip. The betrayal lands there, in that clench, and it’s not metaphorical. Your body knows. It remembers. The jaw, the masseter and temporalis, stiffen into a locked fortress, a physical barricade against the shock. You taste bitter copper, the residue of adrenaline flooding the bloodstream, and your breath hitches, shallow and uneven, the diaphragm unable to drop fully into its soft, round vault beneath the ribs.

The betrayal by a teacher - a figure once trusted to guide, to hold space - settles like a stone lodged deep in the levator scapulae, that stubborn muscle that lifts the shoulder blade toward the neck. The weight of it drags your head forward, a subtle but relentless nod toward shame and disbelief. You want to tell yourself this is just a story, a misalignment of intentions, but the trapezius refuses to release. It knots, a tight coil that radiates tension down the neck and across your shoulders, pulling you into a posture of defensiveness. You can almost smell the faint metallic tang of cortisol, the stress hormone spilling like rain in a thunderstorm you never asked for.

Teachers like Thich Nhat Hanh remind us that the body is the home of the mind’s suffering; Pema Chödrön teaches that discomfort is not an enemy but a doorway. Yet when the one who holds the space becomes the source of your fracture, the somatic landscape shifts. It’s no longer just discomfort but a rupture - a visceral, cellular echo of trust broken. Robert Augustus Masters would say this is the trauma speaking, the nervous system caught in a loop of confusion and alarm. Gabor Maté’s wisdom about the body’s memory of emotional wounds feels acute here: this is not pathology, this is biology.

At 3 a.m., when the house is silent except for the creak of settling wood and the faint rustle of maple leaves scraping against the windowpane, your psoas muscle clenches involuntarily, pulling your pelvis into a tight ball of protection. It’s the body’s ancient signal - a freeze response tucked beneath the surface of your breath, your heartbeat, your skin’s prickling awareness. Herbs like [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20) might soothe the nervous system’s raw edges, but no tincture can unweave the dense web of muscle memory etched by betrayal.

This is the somatic truth of betrayal - not a story of moral failure but the lived, aching experience within your fascia, your tendons, your very cells. It is the body’s cry, loud and unyielding, demanding to be heard before any words can even begin to make sense.

## The Philosophy of Resmaa Menakem and Bessel van der Kolk on How Trauma Lodges in the Body, Applied to the Betrayal of a Teacher

Your body remembers. It remembers the betrayal before your mind can even form the thought. The trapezius tightens - right around the base of your neck - like a clenched fist refusing to let go. Resmaa Menakem, in *My Grandmother’s Hands*, teaches us that trauma is not an event in the light of day but a shadow lodged deep in the tissues of the body, especially the muscles of the neck, the jaw, the chest. When the person who was supposed to guide you, to hold space for your fury, becomes the source of your wound, the levator scapulae contracts protectively, pulling your shoulders up as if to shield you from further harm. This is not metaphor. It is anatomy. It is biology.

Bessel van der Kolk, with his relentless insistence that “the body keeps the score,” shows us how trauma is held in the diaphragm’s uneven rise and fall, how your breath becomes shallow, clipped, like the summer wind cutting through cracked windows on a late August afternoon. When a teacher betrays your trust, your psoas - the muscle that anchors your spine to your legs - locks down. It stiffens in a silent scream of survival, refusing to release because your nervous system is on high alert, waiting for the next blow that may never come. In those moments, you might notice the metallic taste of adrenaline lingering at the back of your throat, or the acrid scent of sweat mingling with the faint trace of chamomile you brewed to calm your nerves but cannot yet swallow.

This is not pathology. This is biology. The jaw clenches - masseter tightening like a taut bowstring - not because you are angry at yourself, but because your body is trying desperately to contain the chaos of conflicting emotions: love, betrayal, rage, grief. Menakem’s work reminds us that these sensations are ancestral, embedded in the very fascia that connects your bones and muscles, passed down through generations as unresolved tension. Your body does not lie. It does not forgive easily. It carries memory in every fiber.

Van der Kolk’s teachings bring us back to the breath, the slow, deliberate inhale and exhale that can begin to rethread the nervous system’s frayed circuits. But when the betrayal is fresh, the breath catches in the throat, the diaphragm spasms unpredictably, and the heart races as if caught in a storm of thunder and rain. It’s in these moments that you must learn to listen - not just with your mind, but with your body’s wisdom. The betrayal of a teacher does not simply break trust; it lodges itself like a shard of glass beneath the skin of your being, sharp and unyielding. Yet, through somatic awareness, through the teachings of Menakem and van der Kolk, you begin the slow, painstaking process of reclaiming your body from that rupture - to uncurl the clenched fists held tight within your muscles, to soften the diaphragm’s hardened edges, and to welcome your breath back home.

## The Danger of Outsourcing Healing to External Authority

You feel it first in the back of your neck - the levator scapulae twitching like a trapped wire, a tight coil winding down your spine. It’s 2:37 a.m., the kind of late that presses your body into itself, making the skin smell faintly of stale sweat and lavender oil you dabbed on hours ago. The ache is familiar - the slow, grinding tension of waiting for someone else to fix what only you can feel. You’ve been taught to outsource your healing, to hand over your body’s messages to external authorities, believing that the map to your own fury lies only in the words of a teacher or the prescription of a potion.

Karla McLaren, who teaches emotional fluency not as a theoretical ideal but as a lived, breathing practice, challenges that very outsourcing. She reminds us that emotions are not problems to be solved by others but languages your body and psyche speak - languages you can learn to understand if you stop relying on someone else to translate for you. Your masseter tightens as you hear this: the jaw clenched in the habit of silencing your own voice, waiting for validation, for permission. This is not pathology; this is biology. The body knows, even when the mind forgets.

McLaren’s philosophy is a radical reclamation of agency. She insists that emotional fluency is a skill you cultivate in the sinews of your own experience, not a doctrine imposed from above. You feel the diaphragm catch in your throat, the breath shallow and uneven, like a river dammed by doubt. When you outsource your healing, you hand over this breath, this muscle, this visceral knowing, to a stranger’s interpretation - sometimes well-meaning, sometimes inflexible, but always external. The danger here is not just misinterpretation but disempowerment: a gradual atrophy of the connective tissue that binds your felt sense to your conscious self.

You’re not broken; you’re not a case study. This is the same teaching echoed by Resmaa Menakem and Bessel van der Kolk: trauma, anger, and grief live in the body’s fascia, in the tight psoas pulling you into fight or flight, in the trapezius that stiffens under the weight of unvoiced rage. Outsourcing healing is like sending a letter in a bottle and waiting for someone across the ocean to read it and send back the answer, while the storm rages inside you.

Instead, McLaren invites you to lean into your own sensations, to become fluent in the subtle shifts of your body’s signals. The prickling warmth behind your eyes, the slow unwinding of the neck’s taut muscles as you breathe into the moment - these are the real teachers. You might reach for herbs like [Herb Pharm Stress Manager (1 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005JW1F1C?tag=spankyspinola-20) or [Gaia Herbs PRO Sleep Formula](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004J32XIG?tag=spankyspinola-20), not as crutches, but as allies in reclaiming your somatic wisdom. The body is your first map and your constant companion. When you stop outsourcing, you find that the anger isn’t something to be tamed or silenced by others - it’s a fierce, living guide pointing you home.

## Reclaiming Your Practice Through the Wisdom of the Body: The Philosophy of Robert Augustus Masters on Fearless Presence

You come to the practice with a jaw clenched so tightly the masseter muscles throb like overworked artisans, a dull ache pulsing behind your ears. This is not just tension - it is memory carved into flesh, fascia woven with old betrayals and the sharp taste of disappointment. Robert Augustus Masters teaches that fearless presence begins here - in the raw, unvarnished terrain of your own body. His philosophy is not about pushing through or intellectualizing discomfort, but about leaning into the somatic truth with a warrior’s gentleness. The body knows. The jaw remembers. To reclaim your practice after a bad teacher, you have to honor the dialogue your tissues are desperate to have.

Fearless presence is not bravado. It is the slow, meticulous unspooling of the diaphragm, the release of the levator scapulae and trapezius that have been bracing since that first sharp word, that cold dismissal. You breathe into the tight, clenched corners of your neck and shoulders, the silent screams of fascia that have thickened like old scar tissue. Masters invites you to settle into this landscape without flinching, without judgment. It is 3 a.m. and the air is thick with the scent of damp earth and the faint, bitter tang of chamomile tea steeping beside you. You feel the subtle quiver of your psoas, the muscle that holds your fight or flight stories like a buried archive. It is not pathology; this is biology.

In this moment, the body is the teacher. You listen with skin and sinew, with the slow unfolding of awareness that a tight trapezius is not just a knot to be undone but a message from your nervous system, a signpost pointing to where your boundaries were ignored, where your fury was deemed ugly. Masters’ fearless presence means to meet these signals without retreat or resistance, to allow the breath to flood into the diaphragm, expanding the ribcage like the unfurling of a dark, ancient petal. Here, you reclaim your practice - not by erasing the past but by folding it into the living, breathing present. The body’s wisdom is the compass; your practice, the map redrawn in muscle and breath.

## Herbs as Allies, Not Crutches

You cradle the rough ceramic cup between your palms, the heat seeping slowly into your skin, radiating warmth up your forearms and settling deep into the trapezius muscle, knotted tight from months of holding tension like a secret no one was meant to know. This is not weakness. This is not failure. This is the body speaking in the language of heaviness and stillness, the diaphragm caught in a shallow cage, the levator scapulae clenched in anticipatory defense. Here, in this moment, the herbs become your allies, not crutches, not magic pills to erase the discomfort, but partners in the conversation you’ve been too afraid to start.

Traditional Chinese Medicine teaches us that stuck qi - this sluggish, turbulent energy lodged somewhere beneath the ribs or behind the sternum - is not a moral flaw but a biological reality. The liver qi, in particular, is prone to stagnation when your fury is denied, when the raw, electric pulse of anger is shamed into silence. You can feel it in the subtlest shifts: a tightening in the masseter muscle along the jawline, the metallic tang in your mouth as adrenaline dips, the subtle pull at the base of your skull where the occipital muscles brace against invisible weight. Herbs like Bupleurum (Chai Hu) and Peony (Bai Shao) are not there to numb or hide these sensations but to move the stuck qi gently, like a skilled somatic guide easing knotted fascia into fluidity.

Susan Weed, one of the great herbalists who refuses to sanitize the body's truths, reminds you that these plants are wild medicine, full of stories and sharp edges, just like your anger. They don’t ask you to be less; they ask you to be real. You drink a tea brewed with fresh ginger, its spicy heat rolling down your esophagus and settling into your solar plexus where the psoas muscles anchor to the spine, tight and braced for the fight you weren’t allowed to have. The ginger's warmth nudges the qi along, like a river breaking through a long-held dam, and you can almost hear the softening in your tissues, the slow uncoiling of the body’s ancient wisdom.

Supporting your nervous system is another form of honoring this body-first messenger. Repeatedly, Resmaa Menakem’s work on racialized trauma and somatic healing echoes in your mind: we must listen to the body’s whispers in the vagus nerve, the deep breath that can unknot the diaphragm, the slow pulse that can reset the heart’s rhythm. Herbs like Reishi mushroom and Schisandra berry, revered in TCM for their adaptogenic properties, don’t promise quick fixes but steady, resilient calm. Their bitter, earthy flavors linger on your tongue, a reminder that healing is a full-sensory experience, grounded in the flesh and bone, not floating somewhere above.

You notice the subtle shifts: the jaw unclenching, the shoulder dropping an inch, the breath deepening into the belly like the tide pulling back to reveal the shore. This is not surrender. This is listening. This is reclaiming practice in the truest sense - not because you are weak but because you are fierce enough to honor what the body remembers. The herbs stand with you, not in front of you, as you begin again.

## The teacher within: Thich Nhat Hanh's embodied mindfulness. How to sit with pain without fleeing or fighting

When you begin to sit with your pain, you might expect an immediate rush of relief or clarity - that’s the way so many have taught us, a quick fix, a cosmic reward for showing up. But Thich Nhat Hanh’s teaching, rooted deeply in embodied mindfulness, asks you instead to become a witness to the raw, unvarnished textures of your suffering. This is not about silencing the pain, nor about pushing it away; it’s about learning to inhabit it fully, as if your body were an ancient temple, every stone and beam alive with memory and meaning. You place your feet flat on the floor, noticing the subtle spread of pressure beneath the arches, how the metatarsals slightly widen under your weight, the way your heels cradle the earth. Your spine lengthens - a slow, deliberate unfurling from the tailbone through the lumbar vertebrae, each intervertebral disc opening like a shutter to light.

Pain, in this practice, is not an enemy to escape but a messenger that arrives with a subtle, insistent knock. You feel it in the masseter muscle, that stubborn clench along the jawline that tightens when you remember the betrayal of a teacher you trusted. It’s a physical echo of the words you swallowed, the boundaries that were crossed. Thich Nhat Hanh invites you to breathe into that exact spot, not to force it open, but to acknowledge its presence. “Hello, my pain,” you might say silently, feeling the vibration of the thought resonate in the hollow of your throat. You notice the way your breath catches, a shallow hitch in the diaphragm, as if your lungs are afraid to take up too much space. This is not pathology; this is biology. Your body is doing exactly what it was designed to do: protect you.

Sitting with pain means noticing the temperature of your skin, the slight dampness at the nape of your neck where the levator scapulae pulls tight, bracing against an invisible threat. It’s 4:15 p.m., the light outside shifting to a bruised, violet gray, and the air in the room feels heavy, smelling faintly of old dust and the sharp, medicinal tang of the [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54ESK?tag=spankyspinola-20) tincture you took earlier. You don’t flee from this heaviness. You don’t fight the urge to run. Instead, you anchor yourself in the sensation of your sit bones pressing into the cushion, the steady, rhythmic pulse of blood in your wrists. You allow the pain to be just one part of the vast landscape of your body, a dark forest within a much larger continent.

This embodied mindfulness is a radical act of reclamation. When you stop fighting the tension in your trapezius, when you stop judging the knot in your psoas, something miraculous begins to happen. The fascia, that intricate web of connective tissue holding your muscles in their rigid patterns, begins to soften. It’s a microscopic yielding, a slow thaw like ice melting under a weak winter sun. You realize that the teacher you’ve been searching for, the one who will never betray you, has been here all along, breathing in the quiet dark of your own chest.

## Trusting the body's wisdom to guide your next steps. The philosophy of Gabor Maté on the non-linear nature of healing

Healing is not a straight line drawn on a map; it is a spiral, a slow circling back to the places that hurt, each time with a little more light, a little more breath. Gabor Maté’s philosophy reminds us that the body’s wisdom is profound, but it speaks in a language of sensation, not logic. When you try to force your healing into a neat, predictable schedule, your body rebels. You might wake up at 2:14 a.m., the air thick and still, smelling faintly of the damp earth outside your window, and feel a sudden, sharp spasm in your psoas. This muscle, the deep core stabilizer that connects your spine to your legs, holds the memory of the urge to flee, the instinct to run from the teacher who betrayed your trust. It tightens now not because you are failing at healing, but because the body is processing a layer of grief it wasn’t ready to touch yesterday.

You must learn to trust this non-linear rhythm. There will be days when your masseter muscle feels soft, your jaw relaxed, and your breath drops easily into the bowl of your pelvis, expanding the diaphragm like a sail catching the wind. You might think, “I am healed.” And then, a week later, a passing comment or a familiar scent might trigger a cascade of tension. The levator scapulae knots up, pulling your shoulders toward your ears in a posture of defense, and the familiar, metallic taste of adrenaline floods your mouth. This is not a setback. This is the body doing its work, unraveling the tightly wound fascia that has held your trauma like a clenched fist. Maté teaches that we must meet these moments with fierce compassion, recognizing that the body is not betraying us; it is finally feeling safe enough to let the pain surface.

Trusting your body means listening to these fluctuations without judgment. It means recognizing that the sudden ache in your trapezius is a request for attention, a plea to slow down and breathe into the space between your shoulder blades. You might reach for a cup of [Gaia Herbs SleepThru (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004GJYTF8?tag=spankyspinola-20) tea, not to numb the sensation, but to support the nervous system as it navigates this turbulent terrain. The warmth of the mug in your hands, the gentle, lemony steam rising to your face - these are anchors, reminding you that you are here, in this moment, safe.

Your next steps are not dictated by a calendar or a curriculum, but by the subtle cues of your own flesh. When the diaphragm feels tight and restricted, you pause. When the breath flows freely, you move forward. This is the ultimate reclamation of your practice: to let the body lead, to honor its hesitations and its sudden leaps of faith. Healing from a bad teacher is not about forgetting what happened; it is about remembering who you are, cell by cell, breath by breath, in the messy, beautiful, non-linear dance of becoming whole again.

## A short embodied practice for releasing tension in the masseter and diaphragm

Find a quiet place where you won’t be disturbed. Sit comfortably, either on a cushion on the floor or in a chair with your feet flat on the ground. Let your spine be tall but not rigid, allowing the natural curves of your back to support you. Close your eyes if that feels safe, or let your gaze rest softly on a spot a few feet in front of you.

Bring your attention to your jaw. Notice the masseter muscle, the thick band of tissue that runs from your cheekbone down to your lower jaw. This is where we often hold our unspoken anger, our silenced boundaries. Don’t try to force it to relax. Instead, just feel it. Is it tight? Does it ache? Imagine you are breathing directly into this muscle. With each inhale, visualize the breath creating space between the fibers of the masseter. With each exhale, invite a tiny, microscopic softening. You might even let your lips part slightly, allowing the lower jaw to hang heavy, surrendering to gravity.

Now, shift your focus down to your diaphragm, the large, dome-shaped muscle that sits at the base of your ribcage. Place one hand gently on your belly, just below your ribs. As you breathe in, feel the belly expand outward, pressing into your hand. This is the diaphragm dropping down, drawing air deep into the lungs. As you breathe out, feel the belly soften and fall back toward the spine. If the breath feels shallow or restricted, don’t judge it. Just notice the sensation of the ribs resisting the expansion.

Stay here for a few minutes, alternating your attention between the softening of the jaw and the deepening of the breath. Inhale, jaw releases. Exhale, belly softens. Inhale, masseter unwinds. Exhale, diaphragm expands. You are not trying to fix anything; you are simply inviting the body to remember its own capacity for ease. When you feel ready, slowly open your eyes, taking a moment to notice the light in the room before you move.

## FAQ

**Q:** How do I know if the tension in my body is from a bad teacher or just normal stress?

**A:** The body’s language is specific, though it takes time to translate. Normal stress often feels diffuse - a general tiredness or a fleeting headache that resolves with rest. The tension from a betrayal, especially by someone in a position of authority, tends to lodge deeply and persistently in specific areas, like the psoas, the masseter, or the levator scapulae. You might notice that this tension flares up when you think about your practice, when you try to meditate, or when you hear certain phrases that the teacher used. It’s a localized, visceral bracing - a physical memory of the exact moment your boundaries were crossed. The psoas, for instance, is the muscle of fight or flight; when it remains chronically tight, it’s often a sign that your nervous system is still responding to a perceived threat, long after the actual danger has passed.

**Q:** Can I still use the techniques I learned from a bad teacher, or do I have to throw everything away?

**A:** This is a deeply personal choice, and your body will give you the answer. When you attempt a technique learned from that teacher, pay close attention to your somatic response. Does your diaphragm constrict? Does your breath become shallow? Do you feel a sudden, sharp ache in your trapezius? If so, your body is telling you that the technique is still entangled with the trauma of the betrayal. It may be necessary to set those practices aside for a while. However, if you can engage with the technique and feel a sense of grounding, if your breath remains full and your jaw relaxed, then you have successfully decoupled the practice from the person who taught it. You are reclaiming the tool as your own. It’s not about the technique itself, but about how your nervous system receives it in the present moment.

**Q:** Why does my body hurt more now that I’ve left the teacher and started trying to heal?

**A:** This is a common and deeply frustrating experience, but it is actually a sign that your healing has begun. When you are in a toxic or abusive environment, your body often goes into a state of functional freeze or hyper-arousal just to survive. The fascia tightens, the muscles brace, and the nervous system suppresses the pain signals so you can keep functioning. When you finally leave and create a safe space for yourself, the body realizes it no longer has to hold that armor in place. As the tension begins to thaw, the stored pain, anger, and grief flood your awareness. The sudden spasms in your psoas or the throbbing in your masseter are not signs of regression; they are the body finally feeling safe enough to process what it has been holding. Gabor Maté emphasizes that this unwinding is non-linear and often uncomfortable, but it is the necessary biology of repair.

**Q:** Are there specific herbs that can help release the physical memory of betrayal?

**A:** Herbs are powerful allies in this work, but they are not erasers. They work best when paired with conscious somatic practice. For the deep, structural tension held in the muscles and fascia, nervines and antispasmodics are particularly supportive. [Pure Encapsulations Daily Calm](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DLCX6T4K?tag=spankyspinola-20) (Scutellaria lateriflora) is excellent for soothing a frayed nervous system and easing the chronic muscle tension that comes from hypervigilance. If the betrayal has left you with a stagnant, simmering anger that feels stuck in your chest or liver area, [FES Yarrow Environmental Solution Spray (2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F3SKA7M?tag=spankyspinola-20) (Chai Hu) can help move that stuck qi, encouraging the emotional and physical release of held frustration. Remember, the herbs are there to support the body’s natural unwinding process, not to force it.

**Q:** How long will it take to fully reclaim my practice?

**A:** There is no timeline for this kind of deep, somatic reclamation. The body does not operate on a schedule; it operates on safety and readiness. Some layers of tension may release quickly, while others - like a deeply held contraction in the psoas or a chronic clench in the masseter - may take months or even years of gentle, persistent attention to fully unwind. The goal is not to rush to a finish line where you are "healed," but to cultivate a daily, living relationship with your body. Every time you notice the tension, breathe into it, and offer it compassion, you are reclaiming your practice. The healing is in the returning, over and over again, to the wisdom of your own flesh.
